comments:
Summary:
This dissertation develops a deep and coherent body of theory connecting learning and optimization. It has two main parts. Part I studies learnability and rates in very general statistical and online frameworks: it shows that uniform convergence/ERM is insufficient in the full general learning setting, proposes stability/AERM characterizations of statistical learnability (including randomized universal learners), and develops sequential analogues of classical complexity measures (sequential Rademacher complexity, sequential covering numbers, fat-shattering) to characterize online learnability and rates. Part II focuses on convex learning/optimization: it analyzes mirror descent (MD) and variants, proving near-optimality and universality of MD for large classes of online convex problems (via martingale type / Banach-space geometry), derives matching lower bounds and oracle-complexity results for statistical and offline convex optimization, and treats many examples (ℓp, Schatten, group norms, max-norm, interpolation norms).

Strengths:
- Ambitious, broad, and technically strong: the thesis ties together learning theory, online minimax analysis, empirical-process tools, and Banach-space geometry to yield novel characterizations and tight (up to logs) optimality statements.
- Fundamental conceptual contributions: failure of uniform convergence in the general setting and the stability/AERM characterization is a notable conceptual advance; sequential Rademacher/covering machinery fills an important gap for non-iid online analysis.
- Unified treatment of convex optimization/learning: the work demonstrates that MD (with appropriate prox) is a universal near-optimal procedure in many settings; the Banach-space viewpoint is powerful and clarifies when/why MD is right.
- Detailed proofs and many examples: the thesis supplies extensive technical detail and applies the results to a wide set of practically relevant norms and problems.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- Presentation density: the material is dense and technical; some proofs are long and could be guided with more intuition and a roadmap at the start of long sections. A high-level summary table of regimes, rates, and which constants/assumptions are needed would greatly improve accessibility.
- Constants and log factors: many results are stated “up to polylog factors” with fairly large numeric constants. While this is typical for deep geometric results, clearer discussion of when these are artifacts of proof techniques vs. intrinsic would be helpful.
- Practical computability/choice of prox: several optimality claims rely on existence of a suitable uniformly convex Ψ; for some norms (e.g. max-norm) the prox implied by the existential construction is impractical. More discussion of computational issues and practically usable prox choices would strenghten impact for practitioners.
- Empirical/algorithmic illustration: the thesis is theoretical; including a short empirical illustration (or pointers to subsequent work) would aid readers in seeing how the theoretical prescriptions translate to algorithms in finite-dimensional problems.

Suggestions:
- Add a concise summary table (problem class / assumptions on H,X / optimal minimax rate / whether MD attains it / comments on complexity and computability of the prox) early in Part II.
- Expand intuitive explanations of the role of martingale type vs. Rademacher type and give a few short worked examples illustrating selection of Ψ in practice.
- When the prox from the construction is impractical (e.g. huge discrete sums for max-norm), discuss alternatives and their trade-offs and point to any follow-up work where such issues are addressed.
- Identify clearly which log factors are artifacts and whether they might be removed by sharpening techniques.

Overall evaluation:
This is an outstanding and substantial thesis that makes multiple, deep contributions to theoretical machine learning and convex optimization. It will be of lasting value to researchers working on learnability, online learning, optimization algorithms, and the geometric/Banach-space aspects of learning. I strongly recommend it for publication/archival posting. (Minor caveats are presentation density and some practical-computation discussion as noted above.)
